title: Learning to listen without preparing my reply
summary: Reflect on the hard skill of listening to someone without already drafting your response, learned during a year living abroad in a Vietnamese city.
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) on the difficult skill of listening to someone without drafting your reply while they speak, and the moment you realised you had been doing the opposite for years. Use the present perfect, the past perfect, a relative clause, and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: During the year I spent teaching English in Hue, a colleague gently pointed out that I tended to start speaking before she had finished her sentence, and at first I…
If you watch most conversations carefully, you will notice that listeners are not actually listening so much as waiting, which is a behaviour I had been guilty of without…
Although learning to listen without preparing my reply has been the slowest skill I have tried to develop as an adult, what I have come to understand is that…

hintVocab.meaningVi: soạn câu trả lời trong đầu

hintVocab.phrase: to be present in a conversation
hintVocab.meaningVi: có mặt thực sự trong cuộc trò chuyện
dành toàn bộ sự chú ý
một khoảng im lặng khó xử
ngồi với một câu hỏi, suy ngẫm về nó
dành thời gian để suy nghĩ

sampleText:
During the year I spent teaching English in Hue, a colleague named Lan gently pointed out, after a staff meeting, that I tended to start speaking before she had finished her sentence. She said it very kindly, the way you might mention that someone has spinach in their teeth, but I felt the comment land with unusual weight. At first I defended myself in my head. I told myself I was just enthusiastic, that I was responding quickly because I cared about the conversation. By the third week of trying — actually trying — to wait until she had finished before I replied, I had to admit she was right. I had not been listening to her, or to anyone, for most of the conversations of my adult life. I had been waiting, with a reply already half-formed, for a small enough gap to insert it.

If you watch most conversations carefully, you will notice that listeners are not actually listening so much as waiting. You can see it in the small movements: an in-breath at the wrong moment, a slight forward lean, the kind of nod that has nothing to do with what is being said. I had been doing all of these things without ever calling it what it was. Although I had read about active listening in articles and had even quoted some of those articles at colleagues, the gap between knowing the idea and actually practising it turned out to be much larger than I had assumed. The first time I genuinely waited until Lan had finished — paused, sat with what she had said, and only then opened my mouth — the conversation felt subtly different. She kept talking. She added a sentence she had been planning to leave out, because she had time to add it. The conversation that came back was not the one I would have got if I had interrupted with my prepared response.

What I have come to think after almost three years of trying to do this is that listening without preparing a reply is one of the slowest skills an adult can try to develop. It does not improve with reading. It improves with the small daily discipline of catching yourself in the middle of a conversation and choosing, against your training, to wait one more second. However, I do not want to claim I have got there. I still catch myself drafting replies when I should be listening, especially in meetings I find boring or with people I find difficult. The skill is not 'I have become a good listener'. It is closer to 'I now notice when I am not listening', which is not the same thing but is the only honest version of progress I can claim. The fact that Lan was willing to mention it gently, over coffee, is one of the more useful gifts a colleague has ever given me.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect inside 'before' for layered timing
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I tended to start speaking before she had finished her sentence' và 'I genuinely waited until Lan had finished' dùng quá khứ hoàn thành sau 'before' / 'until' để nhấn mạnh hành động chưa hoàn tất tại thời điểm chính.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I tended to start speaking before she had finished her sentence' and 'I genuinely waited until Lan had finished' use the past perfect after 'before' / 'until' to emphasise that the action was not yet complete at the main moment.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional inside a comparison: 'the one I would have got if I had interrupted'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The conversation that came back was not the one I would have got if I had interrupted with my prepared response' kết hợp điều kiện loại 3 ('would have + quá khứ phân từ' + 'had + quá khứ phân từ') với mệnh đề quan hệ để so sánh thực tế với điều giả định.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The conversation that came back was not the one I would have got if I had interrupted with my prepared response' combines the third conditional ('would have + past participle' + 'had + past participle') with a relative clause to compare reality with the imagined alternative.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trong năm tôi dạy tiếng Anh ở Huế, một đồng nghiệp tên Lan đã nhẹ nhàng chỉ ra, sau một cuộc họp nhân viên, rằng tôi có xu hướng bắt đầu nói trước khi cô ấy nói xong câu. Cô ấy nói rất tử tế, theo cách bạn có thể nhắc rằng ai đó có rau bina trong răng, nhưng tôi cảm thấy nhận xét đó đáp xuống với sức nặng bất thường. Lúc đầu tôi tự bào chữa cho mình trong đầu. Tôi tự nói với mình rằng tôi chỉ nhiệt tình, rằng tôi đang phản hồi nhanh vì tôi quan tâm đến cuộc trò chuyện. Đến tuần thứ ba của việc cố gắng — thực sự cố gắng — chờ cho đến khi cô ấy nói xong trước khi tôi trả lời, tôi phải thừa nhận cô ấy đã đúng. Tôi đã không lắng nghe cô ấy, hay bất cứ ai, trong phần lớn các cuộc trò chuyện của đời người lớn của tôi. Tôi đã chờ đợi, với câu trả lời đã nửa hình thành, một khoảng đủ nhỏ để chèn nó vào.

Nếu bạn quan sát hầu hết các cuộc trò chuyện kỹ, bạn sẽ nhận thấy rằng người nghe không thực sự đang lắng nghe mà là đang chờ. Bạn có thể thấy điều đó trong các chuyển động nhỏ: một hơi hít vào sai thời điểm, một sự nghiêng nhẹ về phía trước, loại gật đầu không liên quan gì đến điều đang được nói. Tôi đã làm tất cả những điều này mà chưa bao giờ gọi tên nó. Mặc dù tôi đã đọc về lắng nghe chủ động trong các bài báo và thậm chí đã trích dẫn một số bài đó với đồng nghiệp, khoảng cách giữa biết ý tưởng và thực sự thực hành nó hóa ra lớn hơn nhiều so với tôi đã cho rằng. Lần đầu tiên tôi thực sự chờ cho đến khi Lan nói xong — dừng lại, ngồi với điều cô ấy đã nói, và chỉ sau đó mới mở miệng — cuộc trò chuyện cảm thấy hơi khác. Cô ấy tiếp tục nói. Cô ấy thêm một câu mà cô đã định bỏ ra, bởi vì cô có thời gian để thêm nó. Cuộc trò chuyện trở lại không phải là cuộc trò chuyện tôi sẽ có được nếu tôi đã ngắt lời bằng câu trả lời chuẩn bị sẵn.

Điều tôi đã dần nghĩ sau gần ba năm cố làm điều này là lắng nghe mà không chuẩn bị câu trả lời là một trong những kỹ năng chậm nhất mà một người lớn có thể cố phát triển. Nó không cải thiện bằng việc đọc. Nó cải thiện bằng kỷ luật nhỏ hàng ngày của việc bắt gặp chính mình giữa một cuộc trò chuyện và lựa chọn, ngược lại với sự huấn luyện của bạn, chờ thêm một giây. Tuy nhiên, tôi không muốn tuyên bố rằng tôi đã đến nơi. Tôi vẫn bắt gặp mình đang soạn câu trả lời khi tôi nên đang lắng nghe, đặc biệt là trong các cuộc họp tôi thấy chán hoặc với những người tôi thấy khó. Kỹ năng không phải là 'Tôi đã trở thành một người nghe tốt'. Nó gần với 'Giờ tôi nhận ra khi tôi không lắng nghe', không phải là điều giống nhau nhưng là phiên bản trung thực duy nhất của tiến bộ mà tôi có thể tuyên bố. Việc Lan đã sẵn lòng nhắc đến điều đó nhẹ nhàng, qua cà phê, là một trong những món quà hữu ích hơn mà một đồng nghiệp đã từng tặng tôi.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses past perfect correctly after 'before'?
options: I tended to start speaking before she had finished her sentence.
I tended to start speaking before she finished her sentence using past perfect.
I tended to start speaking before she has finished her sentence.
I tended to start speaking before she had finish her sentence.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle' ('finished'). After 'before' it emphasises that her action was not complete.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses past perfect continuous correctly?
options: I have been waiting, with a reply already half-formed, for a small enough gap to insert it.
I had been waiting, with a reply already half-formed, for a small enough gap to insert it.
I had been wait, with a reply already half-formed, for a small enough gap to insert it.
I had being waiting, with a reply already half-formed, for a small enough gap to insert it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the third conditional correctly?
options: The conversation that came back was not the one I would get if I had interrupted.
The conversation that came back was not the one I would have got if I would have interrupted.
The conversation that came back was not the one I would have got if I had interrupted.
The conversation that came back was not the one I would have got if I interrupted.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional: 'would have + past participle' in the main clause; 'had + past participle' in the if-clause.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Would have + past participle' + 'if + had + past participle'.

options: Although I had read about active listening in articles, but the gap between knowing the idea and practising it turned out to be much larger than I had assumed.
Although, I had read about active listening in articles the gap between knowing the idea and practising it turned out to be much larger than I had assumed.
I had read about active listening in articles although however the gap between knowing the idea and practising it turned out to be much larger than I had assumed.
Although I had read about active listening in articles, the gap between knowing the idea and practising it turned out to be much larger than I had assumed.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'not so much … as' correctly?
options: Listeners are not actually listening so much as waiting.
Listeners are not actually listening so much than waiting.
Listeners are not actually listening so much like waiting.
Listeners are not actually listening so much that waiting.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Not so much X as Y' is the standard structure to contrast two things by emphasising Y over X.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'turn out to be + adjective' correctly?
options: The gap turned out be much larger than I had assumed.
The gap turned out to be much larger than I had assumed.
The gap turned out being much larger than I had assumed.
The gap turned out to being much larger than I had assumed.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Turn out + to + base verb' is the standard structure; 'to be + adjective' for the result.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'one of the slowest skills + that + clause' correctly?
options: Listening without preparing a reply is one of slowest skills an adult can try to develop.
Listening without preparing a reply is one of the slowest skill an adult can try to develop.
Listening without preparing a reply is one of the slowest skills an adult can try to develop.
Listening without preparing a reply is one of the most slow skills an adult can try to develop.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'One of the + superlative + plural noun + (that) + clause' is the standard structure. The object relative pronoun can be omitted.

options: The small daily discipline of catch yourself in the middle of a conversation.
The small daily discipline of catching yourself to be in the middle of a conversation.
The small daily discipline of catching you in the middle of a conversation.
The small daily discipline of catching yourself in the middle of a conversation.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Catch + object + -ing' is the standard structure. After a preposition ('of'), use the gerund 'catching'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses present perfect for ongoing experience correctly?
options: What I have come to think after almost three years of trying to do this is that…
What I have came to think after almost three years of trying to do this is that…
What I have come thinking after almost three years of trying to do this is that…
What I has come to think after almost three years of trying to do this is that…
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'have come' + 'to + base verb' for a gradual realisation; 'come' is the past participle (not 'came').

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'one of the more + adjective + plural noun' correctly?
options: It is one of the more useful gift a colleague has ever given me.
It is one of the more useful gifts a colleague has ever given me.
It is one of more useful gifts a colleague has ever given me.
It is one of the most more useful gifts a colleague has ever given me.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'One of the + comparative + plural noun' is grammatical when softening a strong superlative.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'in the middle of + noun' correctly?
options: Catching yourself in middle of a conversation.
Catching yourself at the middle of a conversation.
Catching yourself in the middle of a conversation.
Catching yourself on the middle of a conversation.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In the middle of + noun' is the standard prepositional phrase; the article 'the' is required.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If most listeners are really just waiting to reply, what would change in your relationships if you genuinely waited every time? Would you say less? Would the other person say more? And is there ever a moment when interrupting is actually the kinder thing to do?
